title: Cephalaria decurrens
intro: Cephalaria decurrens, also known as goat's beard or creeping cephalaria, is a flowering plant species in the family Caprifoliaceae, characterized by its tall stems and clusters of small, yellow flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Cephalaria decurrens include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or tincture, and is used to treat conditions such as bronchitis, coughs, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Cephalaria decurrens is classified within the family Caprifoliaceae. This classification is based on its botanical characteristics and relationships with other plant species. Cephalaria decurrens is further classified in the genus Cephalaria, which comprises several species of flowering plants. The exact classification of Cephalaria decurrens may vary depending on the source and the most recent taxonomic research. More specific classification details, such as its order and family rank, can be found in botanical databases and scientific literature.
uses: Cephalaria decurrens is a plant species used in traditional medicine. It has been used to treat various health conditions, including inflammation, fever, and skin diseases. In traditional medicine, Cephalaria decurrens is often used to treat respiratory issues and gastrointestinal problems. Its medicinal uses are primarily based on its bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and phenolic acids.
